Difference between an award and an agreement

content:
About awards

Read our information on awards and working out award coverage.

On this page: What's an award | What's in an award | Working out which award applies | Difference between an award and an agreement | Difference between an award and an employment contract | Employers covered by more than one award | Workplace problems and awards | Changes to awards since 2020 | Tools and resources | Related Information

What's an award

An award (modern award) is a legal document that outlines minimum pay rates and conditions of employment.

Awards don't apply when there's an enterprise agreement in place.

Awards have a coverage clause (usually clause 4) outlining who the award covers and doesn't cover.

In terms of how coverage works, awards usually fall into these categories:
- industry awards
- occupational awards
- industry and occupational awards
- enterprise awards or State reference public sector awards

Industry awards: Most awards have a definition for the industry they cover. An employer will be covered if their business is within the industry defined by the award. An employee will also be covered if there's a classification for them. Examples include the Cleaning Award and the Hospitality Award.

Occupational awards: A small number of awards will cover an employee's occupation. These awards may say they will only cover an employee if there's no industry award for the employee. Examples include the Architects Award and the Clerks Award.

Industry and occupational awards: Some awards contain both industry and occupational coverage rules. Examples include the Health Services Award and the Professional Award.

Enterprise awards or State reference public sector awards: Some employers and their employees are covered by enterprise awards or State reference public sector awards.

What's in an award

Each award has rules (terms) for a specific industry or occupation. These rules cover entitlements like:
- pay rates
- penalty rates and allowances
- hours of work
- breaks
- rosters
- overtime
- dispute resolution processes

If an employee is covered by an award, they get their entitlements from there. This is in addition to the minimum entitlements set out in the National Employment Standards (NES).

Working out which award applies

Every award has information about who it covers and who it doesn't cover.

Award coverage can be based on:
- the employer's industry and the work performed by the employee within the industry
- the employee's occupation, or
- both of the above

Most awards are industry-based and should be generally considered before occupational awards. Occupational awards are considered if there's:
- no industry award
- an industry award but it doesn't have a relevant classification for the employee, or
- an occupational award that's relevant, which doesn't exclude the industry award

Resources to help work out award coverage:
- Pay and Conditions Tool
- Award summaries page
- Reading an award's coverage clause (usually clause 4) and job classifications

Difference between an award and an agreement

An award is different from an enterprise agreement. An enterprise agreement sets out minimum employment conditions but applies to one business or a group of businesses. Enterprise agreements are the result of a bargaining process. The Fair Work Commission (the Commission) reviews and approves enterprise agreements.

Difference between an award and an employment contract

An award covers many employees, while an employment contract is an agreement between an employer and an individual employee. An employment contract sets out terms and conditions of employment. It applies in addition to an award or enterprise agreement. An employment contract can't provide less than the legal minimum entitlements set out in the NES or their award or agreement. A contract can be in writing or verbal.

Employers covered by more than one award

An employer can be covered by more than one award depending on the jobs the employees do. Example: Steve runs a building and construction business with qualified carpenters (Building and Construction Award) and office staff (Clerks Award).

Workplace problems and awards

All awards set out what steps to take if there's an issue about the rules (term) in an award or the NES. This is called the 'dispute resolution' process. In most awards, the first step involves the employee and their employer trying to resolve the issue at the workplace level.

Changes to awards since 2020

The Commission was previously required to review all awards every 4 years. In 2014, the Commission started reviewing most awards. In October 2022, the Commission confirmed it had finalised its review. Most of the updated awards started taking effect at different stages throughout 2020. For many awards, the biggest changes were to the layout and language, and inclusion of rates tables.

key_facts.award_definition: A Modern Award is a legal document outlining minimum pay rates and conditions of employment
key_facts.award_overridden_by: Enterprise agreement (while agreement is in force)
